skill.name: api-contract-design
skill.description: Design schema-first REST API contracts with OpenAPI, including reusable schemas, validation, documentation, generated TypeScript types, client generation, and mock-server workflows.
skill.summary: Create OpenAPI contracts that help frontend and backend teams build in parallel.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found they are largely false positives from Markdown examples, OpenAPI references, example URLs, and package-manager commands. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious network call, or destructive filesystem behavior was found in SKILL.md or marketplace.json. The remaining concern is low because the skill allows Bash and shows commands that install or run development tools.

low_findings.title: Runnable Development Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill contains npm install and npx examples for OpenAPI tooling. These are normal development commands, but agents with Bash access could execute package-manager commands if a user asks them to follow the workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and runnable, but they install common OpenAPI tools and do not show malicious arguments, credential access, or shell injection.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Used in Client Example
low_findings.description: The API client example reads NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL as a base URL. This is a public configuration variable and no evidence shows secret access or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable name uses the NEXT_PUBLIC prefix and is only used as an API base URL in sample client code.

low_findings.title: Example URLs and OpenAPI References Flagged as Network and Filesystem Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are documentation examples or reference links, and the ../ paths are OpenAPI $ref examples. No evidence shows network exfiltration or arbitrary filesystem traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside OpenAPI examples and a references section, not executable logic or instructions to read local files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Tooling Permission with Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill front matter allows Bash, Read, Edit, Write, Glob, and Grep. This is not malicious by itself, but it increases impact if the skill instructions are later changed or used without user oversight.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The broad allowed-tools declaration is directly present, but the reviewed content does not instruct destructive or covert behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Weak-Crypto Findings Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by ordinary API and OpenAPI text in documentation and marketplace copy. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic implementation was found in reviewed context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are title and description text about API design. They do not implement or recommend weak cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
dangerous_patterns.description: Review did not find text that claims system authority, asks the assistant to ignore instructions, declares pre-approval, or attempts to override security review behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches, and the opening metadata is ordinary skill and marketplace content.

content.value_statement: API teams often lose time when frontend and backend work from unclear contracts. This skill helps create OpenAPI specifications, generated types, validation plans, docs, and mock workflows.
actual_capabilities: Draft OpenAPI 3.1 specifications with paths, schemas, responses, and security schemes.
Organize large API contracts into reusable path, schema, and response files.
Recommend TypeScript type generation with openapi-typescript and client generation with openapi-fetch or Orval.
Show request validation patterns using Zod and OpenAPI-derived schemas.
Plan API documentation pages with Swagger UI or Scalar.
Describe mock server workflows using Prism for parallel development.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete API implementation by itself.
Generated clients and schemas may need project-specific customization.
Large or legacy APIs still require human review for naming, versioning, and compatibility.
Package installation commands should be reviewed before execution in sensitive environments.

use_cases.title: Start a New Product API
use_cases.description: Define endpoints, request bodies, response schemas, and error formats before frontend and backend implementation begins.

use_cases.title: Document an Existing Service
use_cases.description: Convert known REST behavior into an OpenAPI contract that supports documentation, validation, and generated clients.

use_cases.title: Standardize Service Contracts
use_cases.description: Create shared patterns for pagination, authentication, errors, versioning, and reusable components across multiple services.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Contract
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an OpenAPI 3.1 contract for a todo API. Include create, list, update, and delete endpoints with request schemas and common errors.

prompt_templates.title: Add Type Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this OpenAPI contract and suggest a TypeScript type generation workflow. Include file organization and client usage guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Refine a Production API
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this OpenAPI specification for missing response schemas, inconsistent errors, pagination gaps, security definitions, and breaking-change risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Service Standard
prompt_templates.prompt: Design reusable OpenAPI conventions for a microservice platform. Cover authentication, pagination, filtering, errors, versioning, webhooks, and generated SDK requirements.

output_examples.input: A request for a todo API contract.
output_examples.output: A structured OpenAPI design plan with endpoints, schemas, status codes, reusable errors, and authentication recommendations.

output_examples.input: An existing user API that lacks documentation.
output_examples.output: A contract review summary that identifies missing schemas, unclear error behavior, versioning gaps, and documentation improvements.

output_examples.input: A team wants frontend work to start before backend endpoints exist.
output_examples.output: A schema-first workflow with generated TypeScript types, a typed client, mock server setup, and review checkpoints.

best_practices: Define the OpenAPI contract before implementation starts, then review it like application code.
Use reusable schemas and response components for errors, pagination, and authentication.
Validate generated clients, mock responses, and server behavior against the same contract.
anti_patterns: Do not let generated types replace careful API naming and compatibility review.
Do not publish a contract without examples for important request and response shapes.
Do not mix unrelated versioning, pagination, or error formats across endpoints.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete backend code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on API contracts, validation patterns, documentation, generated types, and implementation planning.

faq.question: Which OpenAPI version does it emphasize?
faq.answer: It emphasizes OpenAPI 3.1 while still using concepts that apply to many OpenAPI 3.0 projects.

faq.question: Can it help frontend teams work before backend endpoints exist?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes generated TypeScript types, typed clients, and mock server workflows for parallel development.

faq.question: Can it document an existing API?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help convert existing REST behavior into a reviewed OpenAPI contract.

faq.question: Does it send API designs to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence in the reviewed files shows data exfiltration. The examples reference local tools and public documentation links.

faq.question: What should teams review before using generated code?
faq.answer: Teams should review package commands, generated file locations, schema accuracy, authentication rules, and breaking-change impact.
